content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

December 27, 1976

Mr. Peter G. Stone
City Attorney, San Jose
151 West Mission Street
San Jose, California 95110

Dear Mr. Stone:

You seek advice with respect to two situations as follow:

1. A member of the City Council of the City of San Jose
was to testify before a committee of the State Legis-
lature as a representative of the City of San Jose,
authorized to appear before that committee by the
City Council. The subject of the testimony was a
bill being considered by the Legislature which would
directly affect the revenues of the City of San Jose.
It develops that because of the fact that there are
so few flights between San Jose and Sacramento and
the fact that the Councilmember has a busy schedule,
the only way that he can testify is by flying in a
private non-commercial airplane. If this flight was
arranged by a private person who owned the plane,
would the Councilmember be required to report this
plane ride as a gift in his annual statement filed
pursuant to Article 2 of Chapter 7 of the Political
Reform Act?

2. As City Attorney, I am required to appear before the
State Supreme Court representing the City of San Jose
in litigation to which the City is a party. Because
of my schedule, I will be in Southern California just
prior to my appearance and the only means of trans-
portation which will get me to the court on time is
a private airplane. My friend owns such a plane and
offers to fly me to San Francisco so that I can be
on time for my court appearance. If I accept this
offer, do I need to report this ride as a gift to
me under Article 3 of Chapter 7 of the Political
Reform Act, assuming that the Conflict of Interest
Code for my department defines gift in the same
manner as that term is defined in the Political
Reform Act?

Mr. Peter G. Stone
December 27, 1976

Page Two

The Political Reform Act, at Government Code §82028,
defines "gift" as "... any payment to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received".
(Emphasis added.) The term "payment" is defined as follows:

"Payment" means a payment, distribution,
transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift
or other rendering of money, property,
services or anything else of value,
whether tangible or intangible.

Government Code §82044

The Act requires that public officials disclose income,
including gifts of $25 or more in value, on Statements of
Economic Interests. (See Government Code §87207(a)(1), (4),
and 87302(b).) Thus, personal donative use of a private
aircraft by a local official would constitute a gift to the
official within the meaning of §82028, supra, and such a
gift, if valued at $25 or more, would be reportable pursuant
to Government Code §87207 or a Conflict of Interest Code
adopted pursuant to Government Code §87300, et seq.

The facts as you relate them pose the issue of whether,
under the Political Reform Act, the donation of the use of
the aircraft is made to the City or to the official using
the aircraft. Only gifts made to the official are reportable.

You related in telephone conversations subsequent to
the submission of your request for advice that the use of
the aircraft in situations 1 and 2, above, was available
from "friends of the City" to appropriate City officials
who have an official need to utilize the aircraft as
offered. You indicated that the use of the aircraft was
not generally available to City officials but was restricted
to certain high ranking officials, not limited to those men-
tioned in your request for advice. The arrangements outlined
above are not formalized, but rather are generalized rules of
conduct growing out of the ad hoc use or discussed use of the
aircraft.

It is clear that the officials in question receive
significant personal benefit from the service offered by
the "Friends of the City" -- certainly in time and aggre-
vation savings, but, perhaps also, in direct financial
benefits. Such benefits might accrue to the official even
if it is certain that the donation was made to the City and
not to the official.

Mr. Peter G. Stone
December 27, 1976                                                    Page Three


     But, where as here the official is a recipient of the
beneficial service, the policy of the Political Reform Act
requires that the official demonstrate that he or she is
not the donee of the service, in order to avoid the report-
ing requirements of the Act.  Under the arrangement related
above, no such demonstration has been made. 1/

     Thus, the officials in question must report the use of
the aircraft from the "friends of the City" as gifts on
appropriate Statements of Economic Interests at the fair
market value of the service. 2/

     If you disagree with, or have questions regarding, this
advice, please feel free to call me at 916/322-6444.

                                             Sincerely,

                                             [signature]
                                             Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
                                             Conflicts of Interest Division

DLS:glb


_____________________________
1/While staff advice may not be definitive regarding
the standards for demonstrating the difference between
donations to the City and donations to the official, at
minimum there probably must be a showing that:

     (1)  The donor intended to donate the service to the
          City and not to the official;

     (2)  The City exercised the significant decisions
          regarding the use of the service;

     (3)  The service was generally available to City
          personnel on City business without regard to
          official status; and

     (4)  The use of the service was formalized in
          writing or by record which embodied the
          standards set forth above.

Mr. Peter G. Stone
December 27, 1976

Page Four

(continued)

2/Under a Conflict of Interest Code gifts are reportable
only if income is reportable. Income should never be report-
able on the initial statement of designated officials pursuant
to a Conflict of Interest Code.

CITY ATTORNEY

August 25, 1975

Fair Political Practices Commission
1700 R Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Attention: Technical Assistance and Analysis Division

Subject: Request for Advice

Dear Gentlepersons:

I would like your advice on whether reporting under Chapter 7 or any other provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 will be necessary under the following specific circumstances:

1. A member of the City Council of the City of San Jose has been invited to speak at a luncheon meeting at the Chamber of Commerce of the City of San Jose, authorized to appear before them primarily by the City Council. The subject of the address will be considering by the Fair Political Practices Commission, and which would directly affect the revenues of the City of San Jose. It develops that because of the fact that there are so few flights between San Jose and Sacramento and the fact that the Councilmember has a very busy schedule, the only way that they can testify is by flying in a private non-commercial airplane. If this flight was arranged by a private person who owned the plane, would the Councilmember be required to report the plane ride as a gift or financial interest filed pursuant to Article 3 of Chapter 7 or the Political Reform Act?

2. As City Attorney, I am required to appear before the State Supreme Court representing the City of San Jose in litigation to which the City is a party. Because of my schedule, I will be in Southern California just prior to my appearance and the only means of transportation which will get me to the court on time is a private airplane. My friend owns such a plane and offers to fly me to San Francisco so that I can be on time for my court appearance. If I accept this offer, do I need to report this trip as a gift to me under Article 3 of the Political Reform Act, assuming that the District of Intra-Code [illegible] [illegible] direct flight in the same manner as that term is defined in the Political Reform Act?

Fair Political Practices Commission          -2-          August 25, 1975


I would appreciate any assistance you can provide with
respect to clarification of any reporting requirements with
respect to the foregoing questions.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

PALMER G. STONE,
City Attorney

FGS:DHL:sc

CITY OF SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA

151 W. MISSION STREET
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95110
408 277-4458

PETER G. STONE
CITY ATTORNEY

November 18, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

ATTENTION: Technical Assistance and Analysis Division

SUBJECT: Request for Advice

Dear Gentlepersons:

In a letter dated August 25, 1976 I requested your advice with respect to reporting certain matters under Chapter 7 or other applicable provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974. I am attaching a copy of that letter.

I have not yet received a response to my request for advice on this matter.

Please let me know if there is anything I can do or any additional information you require in order to expedite a response to my letter.

Very truly yours,

PETER G. STONE
City Attorney

Attachment

August 25, 1976

1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Attention: Technical Assistance and Analysis Division

Subject:  Request for Advice

Dear Gentlepersons:

     I would like your advice on whether reporting under
Chapter 7 or any other provisions of the Political Reform
Act of 1974 will be necessary under the following specific
circumstances:

     1.  A member of the City Council of the City of San Jose
was to testify before a committee of the State Legislature as
a representative of the City of San Jose, authorized to appear
before that committee by the City Council.  The subject of the
testimony was a bill being considered by the legislature
which would directly affect the revenues of the City of San
Jose.  It develops that because of the fact that there are so
few flights between San Jose and Sacramento and the fact that
the Councilmember has a busy schedule, the only way they
can testify is by flying in a private non-commercial airplane.
If this flight was arranged by a private person who owned the
plane, would the Councilmember be required to report this plane
ride as a gift in their annual statement filed pursuant to
Article 2 of Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act?

     2.  As City Attorney, I am required to appear before the
State Supreme Court representing the City of San Jose in litiga-
tion to which the City is a party.  Because of my schedule, I
will be in Southern California just prior to my appearance and
the only means of transportation which will get me to the court
on time is a private airplane.  My friend owns such a plane and
offers to fly me to San Francisco so that I can be on time for
my court appearance.  If I accept this offer, do I need to report
this ride as a gift to me under Article 3 of Chapter 7 of the
Political Reform Act, assuming that the Conflict of Interest Code
for my department defines gift in the same manner as that term
is defined in the Political Reform Act?

Fair Political Practices Commission                -2-              August 25, 1976


     I would appreciate any assistance you can provide with
respect to clarification of any reporting requirements with
respect to the foregoing questions.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [signature]
                                        PETER G. STONE,
                                        City Attorney

PGS:DML:se

sections.question: 1. A member of the City Council of the City of San Jose was to testify before a committee of the State Legislature as a representative of the City of San Jose, authorized to appear before that committee by the City Council. The subject of the testimony was a bill being considered by the legislature which would directly affect the revenues of the City of San Jose. It develops that because of the fact that there are so few flights between San Jose and Sacramento and the fact that the Councilmember has a busy schedule, the only way that they can testify is by flying a private non-commercial airplane. If this flight was arranged by a private person who owned the plane, would the Councilmember be required to report this plane ride as a gift in their annual statement filed pursuant to Article 2, Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act? 2. As City Attorney, I am required to appear before the State Supreme Court representing the City of San Jose in litigation affecting the City. Because of my schedule, I will be unable to make a timely appearance and will need to use a private plane to arrive on time for the oral argument. Would I be required to report this benefit under Chapter 7 of the Government Code or Article 7 of the Fair Political Practices Act?
sections.question_synthetic: The City Attorney of San Jose seeks advice on whether a City Council member and the City Attorney must report private airplane flights provided to enable their official representation of the City before the State Legislature and State Supreme Court, respectively, as gifts under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both August 25, 1976 initial request and November 18, 1976 follow-up inquiry. This is an incoming request, not an FPPC response. Some text is obscured/unclear due to document quality, but the main questions are identifiable. A partial FPPC response draft appears in the document but is incomplete and fragmentary.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: 1. A member of the City Council of the City of San Jose was to testify before a committee of the State Legislature as a representative of the City of San Jose, authorized to appear before that committee by the City Council. The subject of the testimony was a bill being considered by the legislature which would directly affect the revenues of the City of San Jose. It develops that because of the fact that there are so few flights between San Jose and Sacramento and the fact that the Councilmember has a busy schedule, the only way that they can testify is by flying a private non-commercial airplane. If this flight was arranged by a private person who owned the plane, would the Councilmember be required to report this plane ride as a gift in their annual statement filed pursuant to Article 2, Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act? 2. As City Attorney, I am required to appear before the State Supreme Court representing the City of San Jose in litigation affecting the City. Because of my schedule, I will be unable to make a timely appearance and will need to use a private plane to arrive on time for the oral argument. Would I be required to report this benefit under Chapter 7 of the Government Code or Article 7 of the Fair Political Practices Act?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from Peter G. Stone, City Attorney of San Jose, to the FPPC Technical Assistance and Analysis Division seeking advice on reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act for private airplane flights arranged for city officials performing official duties.
